{{Short description|Mental process in which one rehearses a given action}}
'''Motor imagery''' is a mental process by which an individual rehearses or simulates a given action. It is widely used in sport training as '''mental practice of action''', [[neurological rehabilitation]], and has also been employed as a research paradigm in [[cognitive neuroscience]] and [[cognitive psychology]] to investigate the content and the structure of covert processes (i.e., unconscious) that precede the execution of action.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Decety, J.|author2=Ingvar, D. H.|name-list-style=amp|year=1990|title=Brain structures participating in mental simulation of motor behavior: A neuropsychological interpretation|journal=Acta Psychologica|volume=73|issue=1|pages=13–34|pmid=2180254|doi=10.1016/0001-6918(90)90056-L}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author1=Decety, J.|author2=Stevens, J.|name-list-style=amp|year=2009|chapter=Action representation and its role in social interaction|editor1=K.D. Markman|editor2=W.M.P. Klein |editor3= J.A. Suhr|title=The Handbook of Imagination and Mental Simulation|location=New York|publisher=Psychology Press|isbn=978-1-84169-887-8}}</ref> In some medical, musical, and athletic contexts, when paired with physical rehearsal, mental rehearsal can be as effective as pure physical rehearsal (practice) of an action.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Kappes|first1=Heather Barry|last2=Morewedge|first2=Carey K.|date=2016-07-01|title=Mental Simulation as Substitute for Experience|journal=Social and Personality Psychology Compass|language=en|volume=10|issue=7|pages=405–420|doi=10.1111/spc3.12257|s2cid=4823141 |issn=1751-9004|url=https://researchonline.lse.ac.uk/id/eprint/66305/}}</ref>
==Definition==
Motor imagery can be defined as a dynamic state during which an individual mentally simulates a physical action. This type of phenomenal experience implies that the subject feels themselves performing the action.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Decety, J.|year=1996|title=Do imagined and executed actions share the same neural substrate? |journal=Cognitive Brain Research|volume=3|issue=2|pages=87–93|pmid=8713549|doi=10.1016/0926-6410(95)00033-X|citeseerx=10.1.1.211.8747}}</ref> It corresponds to the so-called internal imagery (or first person perspective) of [[sport psychology|sport psychologists]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Mahoney, M..J.|author2= Avener, M.|name-list-style=amp|year=1987|title=Psychology of the elite athlete|journal=Cognitive Therapy Research|volume=1|issue=2|pages= 135–141|doi=10.1007/BF01173634|s2cid= 39561309}}</ref>

==Mental practice of action==
'''Mental practice''' refers to use of visuo-motor imagery with the purpose of improving motor behavior. Visuo-motor imagery requires the use of one's [[imagination]] to simulate an action, without physical movement. It has come to the fore due to the relevance of imagery in enhancing sports and surgical performance.<ref name=":0" />

===Sports===
Mental practice, when combined with physical practice, can be beneficial to beginners learning a sport, but even more helpful to professionals looking to enhance their skills.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Frank|first1=Cornelia|last2=Land|first2=William M.|last3=Popp|first3=Carmen|last4=Schack|first4=Thomas|date=2014-04-17|title=Mental Representation and Mental Practice: Experimental Investigation on the Functional Links between Motor Memory and Motor Imagery|journal=PLOS ONE|volume=9|issue=4|article-number=e95175|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0095175|issn=1932-6203|pmc=3990621|pmid=24743576|bibcode=2014PLoSO...995175F|doi-access=free}}</ref>&nbsp;Physical practice generates the physical feedback necessary to improve, while mental practice creates a cognitive process physical practice cannot easily replicate.<ref name=":1">{{cite journal|author=Cocks, Margaret|display-authors=etal|title=What surgeons can learn from athletes: mental practice in sports and surgery|journal=Journal of Surgical Education|volume=71|issue=2|pages=262–9|year=2014|doi= 10.1016/j.jsurg.2013.07.002|pmid=24602719}}</ref>

=== Medicine ===
When surgeons and other medical practitioners mentally rehearse procedures along with their physical practice, it produces the same results as physical rehearsal, but costs much less. But unlike its use in sports, to improve a skill, mental practice is used in medicine as a form of stress reduction before operations.<ref name=":1" />

=== Music ===
Mental practice is a technique used in music as well. Professional musicians may use mental practice when they are away from their instrument or unable to physically practice due to an injury. Studies show that a combination of physical and mental practice can provide improvement in mastering a piece equal to physical practice alone.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bernardi|first1=Nicolò Francesco|last2=Schories|first2=Alexander|last3=Jabusch|first3=Hans-Christian|last4=Colombo|first4=Barbara|last5=Altenmüller|first5=Eckart|date=2013|title=Mental Practice in Music Memorization: An Ecological-Empirical Study|url=http://ego.psych.mcgill.ca/labs/mcl/pdf/nicolo2.pdf|journal=Music Perception|volume=30|issue=3|pages=275–287|doi=10.1525/mp.2012.30.3.275}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Pascual-Leone|first1=Alvaro|last2=Dang|first2=Nguyet|last3=Cohen|first3=Leonardo G.|last4=Brasil-Neto|first4=Joaquim P.|last5=Cammarota|first5=Angel|last6=Hallett|first6=Mark|date=September 1995|title=Modulation of Muscle Response Evoked by Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation During the Acquisition of New Fine Motor Skills|url=http://www.chrisdonnellymusic.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/02/Pascual-Leone1995.pdf|journal=Journal of Neurophysiology|volume=74|issue=3|pages=1037–1043|doi=10.1152/jn.1995.74.3.1037|pmid=7500130}}</ref> This is because mental practice causes neuron growth that mirrors growth caused by physical practice. And there is precedent: [[Vladimir Horowitz]] and [[Arthur Rubinstein]], among others, supplemented their physical practice with mental rehearsal.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Pascual-Leone|first=Alvaro|date=June 2001|title=The Brain That Plays Music and Is Changed by It|journal=The Biological Foundations of Music|volume=930|issue=1|pages=315–329|doi=10.1111/j.1749-6632.2001.tb05741.x|pmid=11458838|bibcode=2001NYASA.930..315P|s2cid=30266285 }}</ref>

===Motor deficits===
Mental practice has been used to rehabilitate motor deficits in a variety of neurological disorders.<ref name=Jackson>{{cite journal|author1=Jackson, P.L.|author2=Lafleur, M.|author3=Malouin, F.|author4=Richards, C.|author5=Doyon, J.|name-list-style=amp|year=2001|title=Potential role of mental practice using motor imagery in neurologic rehabilitation|journal=Archives of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation|volume=82|issue=8|pages=1133–41|pmid=11494195|doi=10.1053/apmr.2001.24286}}</ref> Mental practice of action seems to improve balance in individuals with [[multiple sclerosis]] and in elderly women.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Fansler, C. L.|author2=Poff, C. L.|author3=Shepard, K. F.|name-list-style=amp|year=1985|title=Effects of mental practice on balance in elderly women|journal=Physical Therapy|volume=65|issue=9|pages=1332–8|pmid=4034667|citeseerx=10.1.1.1024.7951|doi=10.1093/ptj/65.9.1332}}</ref> For instance, mental practice has been used with success in combination with actual practice to rehabilitate motor deficits in a patient with sub-acute stroke.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Page, S. J.|author2=Levine, P.|author3=Sisto, S. A.|author4=Johnston, M. V.|name-list-style=amp|year=2001|title=Mental practice combined with physical practice for upper-limb motor deficit in subacute stroke|journal=Physical Therapy|volume=81|issue=8|pages=1455–62|pmid=11509075|doi=10.1093/ptj/81.8.1455|doi-access=free}}</ref>  Several studies have also shown improvement in strength, function, and use of both upper and lower extremities in chronic stroke.

Some studies evaluated the effect of MI in gait rehabilitation after stroke however there was very low‐certainty evidence that motor imagery is more beneficial for improving gait (walking speed), motor function and functional mobility compared to other therapies, placebo or no intervention.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Silva|first1=Stephano|last2=Borges|first2=Lorenna RDM|last3=Santiago|first3=Lorenna|last4=Lucena|first4=Larissa|last5=Lindquist|first5=Ana R|last6=Ribeiro|first6=Tatiana|date=2020-09-24|title=Motor imagery for gait rehabilitation after stroke|journal=Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews|volume=2020|issue=9|article-number=CD013019 |doi=10.1002/14651858.cd013019.pub2|issn=1465-1858|pmc=8094749|pmid=32970328}}</ref> Additionally, there was insufficient scientific evidence to assess the influence of MI on the dependence on personal assistance and walking endurance.<ref name=":2" />

==Functional equivalence to motor preparation==
[[Image:Motor imagery3.jpg|thumb|Converging empirical evidence indicates a functional equivalence between action execution and motor imagery.]]
Motor imagery has been studied using the classical methods of [[introspection]] and [[mental chronometry]]. These methods have revealed that motor images retain many of the properties, in terms of temporal regularities, programming rules and biomechanical constraints, which are observed in the corresponding real action when it comes to execution. For instance, in an experiment participants were instructed to walk mentally through gates of a given apparent width positioned at different apparent distances. The gates were presented to the participants with a 3-D visual display (a virtual reality helmet) which involved no calibration with external cues and no possibility for the subject to refer to a known environment. Participants were asked to indicate the time they started walking and the time they passed through the gate. Mental walking time was found to increase with increasing gate distance and decreasing gate width. Thus, it took the participant longer to walk mentally through a narrow gate than to walk through a larger gate placed at the same distance.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Decety, J.|author2=Jeannerod, M.|name-list-style=amp|year=1996|title=Mentally simulated movements in virtual reality: does Fitt's law hold in motor imagery?|journal=Behavioural Brain Research|volume=72|issue=1–2|pages=127–34|citeseerx=10.1.1.299.9473|pmid=8788865|doi=10.1016/0166-4328(96)00141-6|s2cid=4055829}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Decety, J.|author2=Jeannerod, M.|author3=Prablanc, C.|name-list-style=amp|year=1989|title=The timing of mentally represented actions|journal=Behavioural Brain Research|volume=34|issue=1–2|pages=35–42|pmid=2765170|doi=10.1016/S0166-4328(89)80088-9|citeseerx=10.1.1.212.3957|s2cid=4007912}}</ref> This finding led neurophysiologists [[Marc Jeannerod]] and [[Jean Decety]] to propose that there is a similarity in mental states between action simulation and execution.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Decety, J.|author2=Jeannerod, M.|name-list-style=amp|year=1995|title=L'imagerie mentale et son substrat neurologique|journal=Revue Neurologique|volume=151|pages=474–479}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Jeannerod, M.|year=1994|title=The representing brain: Neural correlates of motor intention and imagery|journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences|volume=17|issue=2|pages=187–202|url=http://users.abo.fi/jtuomain/EMCL/articles/additional/Jeannerod1994.pdf|doi=10.1017/S0140525X00034026|s2cid=40004903 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Decety, J.|year=1996|title=Neural representations for action|journal=Reviews in the Neurosciences|volume=7|issue=4|pages=285–97|pmid=9044503|doi=10.1515/REVNEURO.1996.7.4.285|s2cid=45472054}}</ref>

The functional equivalence between action and imagination goes beyond motor movements. For instance similar cortical networks mediate music performance and music imagery in pianists.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Meister, I.G.|author2=Krings, T.|author3=Foltys, H.|author4=Boroojerdi, B.|author5=Muller, M.|author6=Topper, R. R.|author7=Thron, A.|name-list-style=amp|year=2004|title=Playing piano in the mind - an fMRI study on music imagery and performance in pianists|journal=Cognitive Brain Research|volume=19|issue=3|pages=219–28|pmid=15062860|doi=10.1016/j.cogbrainres.2003.12.005|citeseerx=10.1.1.473.1034}}</ref>

==Physiology==
[[Image:MI fMRI.jpg|thumb|left|Activation in the motor cortex during motor imagery amounts about 30 % of the level observed during actual performance; Roth et al., 1996.]] A large number of [[functional neuroimaging]] studies have demonstrated that motor imagery is associated with the specific activation of the neural circuits involved in the early stage of motor control (i.e., motor programming). This circuits includes the [[supplementary motor area]], the primary [[motor cortex]], the inferior [[parietal cortex]], the [[basal ganglia]], and the [[cerebellum]].<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Decety, J.|author2=Perani, D.|author3=Jeannerod, M.|author4=Bettinardi, V.|author5=Tadary, B.|author6=Woods, R.|display-authors=etal|year=1994|title=Mapping motor representations with PET|journal=Nature|volume=371|issue=6498|pages=600–2|doi=10.1038/371600a0|pmid=7935791|s2cid=4243701}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Roth, M.|author2=Decety, J.|display-authors=etal|year=1996|title=Possible involvement of primary motor cortex in mentally simulated movement: A functional magnetic resonance imaging study|journal=NeuroReport|volume=7|issue=7|pages=1280–4|pmid=8817549|doi=10.1097/00001756-199605170-00012 }}</ref> Such physiological data gives strong support about common neural mechanisms of imagery and motor preparation.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Jeannerod, M.|year=2001|title=Neural simulation of action: A unifying mechanism for motor cognition|journal=NeuroImage|volume=14|issue=1 Pt 2|pages=S103–9|pmid=11373140|doi= 10.1006/nimg.2001.0832|citeseerx=10.1.1.212.520|s2cid=16052076}}</ref>

Measurements of [[cardiac]] and respiratory activity during motor imagery and during actual motor performance revealed a covariation of heart rate and pulmonary ventilation with the degree of imagined effort.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Decety, J.|author2=Jeannerod, M.|author3=Durozard, D.|author4=Baverel, G.|name-list-style=amp|year=1993|title=Central activation of autonomic effectors during mental simulation of motor actions in man|journal=Journal of Physiology|volume=461|issue=1|pages=549–63|pmc=1175272|pmid=8102402|doi=10.1113/jphysiol.1993.sp019528}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Wang, Y.|author2=Morgan, W.P.|name-list-style=amp|year=1992|title=The effect of imagery perspectives on the psychophysiological responses to imagined exercise|journal=Behavioural Brain Research|volume=52|issue=2|pages=167–74|doi=10.1016/S0166-4328(05)80227-X|pmid=1294196|s2cid=4017522}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Wuyam, B.|author2=Moosavi, S.H.|author3=Decety, J.|author4=Adams, L.|author5=Lansing, R.W.|author6=Guz, A.|name-list-style=amp|year=1995|title=Imagination of dynamic exercise produced ventilatory responses which were more apparent in competitive sportsmen|journal=Journal of Physiology|volume=482|issue=Pt 3|pages=713–24|pmid=7738860|pmc=1157796|doi=10.1113/jphysiol.1995.sp020554}}</ref>
Motor imagery activates motor pathways. Muscular activity often increases with respect to rest, during motor imagery. When this is the case, EMG activity is limited to those muscles that participate in the simulated action and tends to be proportional to the amount of imagined effort.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Wehner, T.|author2=Vogt, S.|author3=Stadler, M.|name-list-style=amp|year=1984|title=Task-specific EMG characteristics during mental training|journal=Psychological Research|volume=46|issue=4|pages=389–401|doi=10.1007/BF00309071|pmid=6522565|s2cid=38553185}}</ref>

==Effects==
Motor imagery is now widely used as a technique to enhance motor [[learning]] and to improve [[neurological rehabilitation]] in patients after [[stroke]]. Its effectiveness has been demonstrated in musicians.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Lotze, M.|author2=Scheler, G.|display-authors=etal|year=2003|title=The musician's brain: functional imaging of amateurs and professionals during performance and imagery|journal=NeuroImage|volume=20|issue=3|pages=1817–29|pmid=14642491|doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2003.07.018|s2cid=1703363|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/8605838}}</ref>

* On motor learning: Motor imagery is an accepted procedure in the preparation of athletes. Such practice usually covers a warming up period, [[Relaxation (psychology)|relaxation]] and concentration, and then mental simulation of the specific movement.<ref>{{cite book|author=Suinn, R.M.|year=1984|chapter=Imagery and sports|editor1=W.F. Straub|editor2=J. M. Williams |title=Cognitive Sport Psychology|location=Lansing, NY|publisher= Sport Science Associates}}</ref>
* In neurological rehabilitation: Since pioneering work by Dr. Stephen Page in 2000,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Page|first=Stephen J.|date=2000|title=Imagery improves upper extremity motor function in chronic stroke patients: A pilot study.|journal=Occupational Therapy Journal of Research|volume=20|issue=3|pages=200–215|doi=10.1177/153944920002000304|s2cid=143590955}}</ref> there has been accumulating evidence suggesting that motor imagery provides additional benefits to conventional [[physiotherapy]] or occupational therapy.<ref name=Jackson/> A recent review on four randomized controlled trials indicates that there is modest evidence supporting the additional benefit of motor imagery compared to only conventional physiotherapy in patients with stroke.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Zimmermann-Schlatter, A.|display-authors=etal|year=2008|title=Efficacy of motor imagery in post-stroke rehabilitation: a systematic review|journal=Journal of Neuroengineering and Rehabilitation|volume=5|page=8|doi=10.1186/1743-0003-5-8|pmc=2279137|pmid=18341687 |doi-access=free }}</ref> These authors concluded that motor imagery appears to be an attractive treatment opinion, easy to learn and to apply and the intervention is neither physically exhausting nor harmful. Therefore, motor imagery may generate additional benefit for patients.
* Motor imagery can act as a [[Substitute good|substitute]] for the imagined behavior, producing similar effects on cognition and behavior.<ref name=":0" /> The repeated simulated consumption of a food, for example, can reduce subsequent actual consumption of that food.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Morewedge|first1=Carey K.|last2=Huh|first2=Young Eun|last3=Vosgerau|first3=Joachim|date=2010-12-10|title=Thought for Food: Imagined Consumption Reduces Actual Consumption|journal=Science|volume=330|issue=6010|pages=1530–1533|doi=10.1126/science.1195701|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21148388|bibcode=2010Sci...330.1530M|s2cid=5355769}}</ref>

==Simulation and understanding mental states==
Motor imagery is close to the notion of simulation used in cognitive and [[social neuroscience]] to account for different processes. An individual who is engaging in simulation may replay his own past experience in order to extract from it pleasurable, motivational or strictly informational properties.  Such a view was clearly described by the Swedish physiologist Hesslow.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Hesslow, G.|year=2002|title=Conscious thought as simulation of behavior and perception|journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences|volume=6|issue=6|pages=242–247|pmid=12039605|doi=10.1016/S1364-6613(02)01913-7|s2cid=14412895}}</ref>  For this author, the [[simulation hypothesis]] states that thinking consists of simulated interaction with the environment, and rests on the following three core assumptions: (1) Simulation of actions: we can activate motor structures of the brain in a way that resembles activity during a normal action but does not cause any overt movement; (2) Simulation of perception: imagining perceiving something is essentially the same as actually perceiving it, only the perceptual activity is generated by the brain itself rather than by external stimuli; (3) Anticipation: there exist associative mechanisms that enable both behavioral and perceptual activity to elicit other perceptual activity in the sensory areas of the brain.  Most importantly, a simulated action can elicit perceptual activity that resembles the activity that would have occurred if the action had actually been performed.

Mental simulation may also be a representational tool to understand the self and others. [[Philosophy of mind]] and developmental psychology also draw on simulation to explain our capacity to mentalize, i.e., to understand mental states (intentions, desires, feelings, and beliefs) of others (aka [[theory of mind]]).  In this context, the basic idea of simulation is that the attributor attempts to mimic the mental activity of the target by using his own psychological resources.<ref>{{cite book|author=Goldman, A.I.|year=2002|chapter=Simulation theory and mental concepts|editor1=J. Dokic |editor2=J. Proust|name-list-style=amp|title=Simulation and Knowledge of Action|volume=45|pages=1–19|doi=10.1075/aicr.45.02gol|series=Advances in Consciousness Research|isbn=978-90-272-5169-5}}</ref>  In order to understand the mental state of another when observing the other acting, the individual imagines herself/himself performing the same action, a covert simulation that does not lead to an overt behavior.  One critical aspect of the simulation theory of mind is the idea that in trying to impute mental states to others, an attributor has to set aside her own current mental states, and substitutes those of the target.<ref>{{cite book|author=Goldman, A.I.|year=2005|chapter=Imitation, mind reading, and simulation|editor1=S. Hurley|editor2= N. Chatert|name-list-style=amp|title=Perspective on Imitation, from Neuroscience to Social Science, Volume 2|pages=79–93|location=Cambridge|publisher=MIT Press}}</ref>

* [[Creative visualization]]
* [[Empathy]]
* [[Motor cognition]]
* [[Motor planning]]
